text: I'm grabbing this image, grabbing this image, and I'm just saying, in here, with the agent, I'm saying, make the worm coming out of the sand.

text: And now the agent is going to say, okay, so then it takes my little instruction, and then it gives its own instruction to the model.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: which is Nano Banana Pro, in this case, right? I'm instructing the agent, the agent is an orchestrator, it's then instructing its executor agent to then go in and really enhance that prompt. And, you know, one thing I say is, like, you don't have to worry about

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: how the agent does it, you just have to worry about telling it what you want it to do. And the way I always kind of instruct people to think about this is, like.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: If you're a creative director, or you're a film director, you have your crew that you trust. You know that they have all the skills necessary to

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: to make things happen. You don't necessarily tell them… micromanage them and tell them exactly how to do their job in every single way. You're just the director, you're saying, I just want you to make the worm coming out of the sand. It's a given that it should look good. Like, it should look realistic, it should look… but you don't have to tell them that, because you have a certain level of trust for your production team that they know what they're doing. Same thing goes here. This is like…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: pretend like this whole canvas is the office next door, where your production team is at, right? And you're the creative director, you're bringing them over all these different assets and these different instructions, you set it down on their desk, this is the desk.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And then you're telling them, look, this is what I want. I want you to do this, okay? I'll be back.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: then your production team does what they do. You don't need to tell them how. You're not a micromanager, you're a director. You have trust for them. So then they make their stuff, and then they come back to you with an output, say, hey, what do you think about this?

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Then you say, oh, you know what, like, I forgot to tell you this, this is what I need now. Actually, I need this other change. Do this a little bit differently, whatever. Something that I also get in the habit of doing is, like.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: I used to be a… and I used to be a creative director in multiple settings, and

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: I, a lot of times, would forget to ask them my team questions before I left the room.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: For them to complete the task.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And then I come back, and when they have their final work, and they present it to me, I'm like, shit, I forgot to tell you this one thing. So instead of having to go back, I tell the agent, I tell my team up front, before I leave the room.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Hey guys, do you have any questions for me before I leave and let you, you know, leave you to it? Before I leave, do you have any questions?

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: that I need to answer for you before you start your work? And usually the answer is yes, actually. There's these couple questions that I want to ask you that, you know, just to clarify before I start working on this.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: What should the whatever, how do you want this to happen? What is, you know, minute details. You'll find out. If you do the same thing with the agent, it'll do the same thing. It'll ask you… if you don't ask questions, it won't. It won't actually… it'll assume your instructions are complete. So, I ask the questions, like, hey, before you start.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Please…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: let me know if you have any questions for me, anything I can clarify before you start generating this… this task, right? So…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Really good practice to get into, is like, you know, you just… first of all, you tell them what to do.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: You don't tell them how. It knows how. But make sure you're clear in what you are asking it to do, you know? Because it'll take your instructions as, like, oh, this is what I'm gonna do.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And if you… if your instructions are not complete, if you didn't give full context, if you didn't give the details you needed, then it's gonna… it's gonna… it's going to create those details for you.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And you can see that, obviously, when I… when I show this.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: system prompt, right? It's gonna do all these different things here.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And, you can see kind of what it's doing.

speaker: Artur Monteiro
text: what's the magic word so the model understands? And, like you're seeing right here in the visual example, it's just directing, saying what you want. It's… we… this product is aiming for people not having to find a secret word, it's more used in natural language.

speaker: Artur Monteiro
text: And then the model will make this interpretation and send to the chosen image model. It could be UniOne, in this case, Nano Banana. So, there's no magic word.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: You know, what's also really, really interesting is that you can throttle your position back and forth.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: you can be a creative director, and a director-level, you know, kind of like high-level instructions, no problem. But whenever you want to, you can also be a micromanager, if you really want to. You can step into the production shoes as well. So, like, for example, I'll go back to that car.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Like… I… I am… I do come from, like, a prompt engineering background, because I started…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: you know, prompting systems when they first came out, and first you didn't have a Gentic, like, prompt enhancements so clear. And so, for example, in this case, like, you can also tell it exactly what to do.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Right? Like, I can also just say, like, just turn… make this into a black car on a racetrack. But I can also go in there and give it a very intense, controlling prompt, if I really want to.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: So you can throttle that back and forth. And then you can even… you can do this inside of, like, the modo, where it's, like, the right-click modo, and just say, make… make a… make an image here, or you can do it from here, which, like, when you're making an image here.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: you can type in a very, very detailed prompt in this case, right? Because you don't have the agent. You're not leaning on the agent to do this here, in that case. But when you are leaning on the agent to do something, and you're just, you know, selecting this, and then telling it, you know, just do this.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: then the agent is obviously going to go in there and, you know, enhance your prompt. But you can also tell it, do not enhance

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: my prompt at all. You can… you can give it those directions, and then whatever prompt you gave it here, like, I can just say, make the car black, and don't…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: change anything else, whatever. My god, the spelling mistakes. Don't change, anything else

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Then I can also say, do not enhance my prompt at all. And it will do that, and you'll see it'll come out, like, not very good, because…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: you're just not giving the agent a chance to orchestrate its other executor agents, which is really what it does best. But you have the choice. You have the choice of being a director, or being a production artist. And you can throttle that back and forth all the time.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: You'll just… you'll see, as you go on, that as long as you're giving full instructions, as long as you're treating the agent as if, literally, it's your production artist, or your production team.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: and you are treating it as almost like a real production team, like the way you talk to them, the assets you give them, setting them up for success. If you've ever been in the shoes of an art director, creative director, film producer, or film director.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Like, that's the best way that this… this thing works, is when you are in that director's seat.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And do the same things you would do for your team

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: being a director. You set them up for success. You give them all the assets they need, you give them the instructions you need, and then you ask them questions that, just to make sure they understand your instructions and your direction.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And then you let them do their thing. And then when it comes back, you might have to give them some feedback or revisions. The worse your instructions are, the more revisions you're gonna have to do. That's the same way in real life. It's like, you give crappy instructions, you give zero context.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: to your team of workers, they come back with something that doesn't make any sense, and then you're gonna have to give more feedback to them, and be like, no, you guys gotta do this again, and this is what I should have told you at the beginning. I forgot to tell you that, like, all this and that, and this and the other. So…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: That, to me, is, like, the key of working with this, is, again, the best analogy I can give you, this entire canvas, this entire board, is the agent's office. It's your production team's office. You are bringing them assets, you're bringing them a folder of stuff.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: and then you are on a whiteboard, you're giving them the directions of what to do with all those assets, that's gonna be your prompt in your agent chat window. And then you're gonna ask them, do you have any other questions for me before I leave the room and go take care of a bunch of other stuff that I have to do? And you get started.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: You know, and then it'll ask, yes, I have a couple questions, okay, before you leave, like, please answer this. And then you can leave, and let them do their job, right? And don't worry about how the sausage is made, how every ingredient is made. You just get the sausage back, and you taste it, and if it's amazing, it's amazing. If it's not good, then, you know, you gotta go back and do a couple revisions.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: But that's the same way that you work in real life.

speaker: Davicho Barona

text: And… Try to be insightful as to, like.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: to… and I'm not just saying it because I don't want you to use some other product, I want you to use our product. Like, no, that's not actually why I'm asking you this to do this, but because there's so much more context that the agent here has about its own tools.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And stuff. And just by the way, under the hood here are the top LLM models here. Not… Perplexity's leaning on, probably, ChatGPT. This is leaning on… without me revealing too much information that I'm not supposed to talk about, this is leaning on models that are better than that.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: So, and it has the context of all the tools that it can use. For example.

text: text. Not only read it, but write it. And it has access to all Google fonts. So, if you go to Google, and Google, show me a list of Google Fonts, in fact, you can do that in here, too. You can say, you know, tell me all the Google fonts you have access to.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: it'll give you a full list of all the fonts it has access to, and then you can also say, like, okay, show me, like, how you would write whatever word in this font. I'll give you an example. So.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: rather, copy block paragraph formatting, but…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: In this case, and you can see that this font is very blurry in this case, right? That's because I used Nano Banana Pro to just relay out my original ad. Same thing here, using Nano Banana Pro all the way through the font.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: like, looking copy block. Now, when I go down to the parts where it actually used remote motion.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: orientation, right? This is like a 9 to 16 coming from this… it's not quite a square, this may be 4 to 5, maybe not even, maybe very much custom. And so same… same thing here. As you can see in this case.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: it had to stretch this out quite a bit, and so it had to rewrite this, and it rewrote that using remotion. So you could literally ask it to use Remotion to write that again.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And that is something that I swear by now, is actually using Remotion versus Nano Banana Pro, or whatever the image model is, to recreate the text, because as soon as you do use Remotion.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: In this case, it recreated the text of my headline. As you can see, it had to do that, but this is not a font. This is a custom font that I made for this particular campaign years ago, but you can see the original here.

text: Obviously, it has a lot more of these little stars than this version here, because this, it had to, like, recreate

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: like an image, basically. But in this case, it had to recreate it, actually writing it with remotion, which it gets 100% right. Even this sideways

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: text here, which is really hard to do. If you see, if you look at it with the… with the nanobanana.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: versions, it's all garbled. It doesn't get it. It truly does not understand how to do that. But as soon as you introduce Remotion to it, you'll see a massive, massive difference. This is how it makes captions, this is how it makes a lot of programmatic,

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: text editing, like, if you ever did… if you ever made websites with… or used CSS in any way, that is kind of the same context here. You can give it, those…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: kind of very detailed instructions of how to do something, how to style it, how to space it, etc, by, by using remotion.

speaker: Eric David
text: So is remotion an edit tool or an image generator?

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Remotion is an edit tool. It's actually more oriented towards video and motion, but also text and styling.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: So you… and it's… it's kind of a hidden tool. It's not like… you can ask it, you know, you can ask it here, like, how do you… how do… you know, you can see it here, actually, in one of these answers here. So this was the answer, remember I asked it, tell me all the Google fonts you have access to. It was like, great question, let me check.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And then it said, here's what I do. He said, I don't have a fixed, preloaded list of Google's fonts to share with you.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Here's what I do know about fonts across platform tools, and it says Remotion Video Rendering. When composing videos, text overlays, fonts are specified via CSS font family in the code. I'll go back to another board here to show you.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: This is a board about video captioning here, which I did… this is what I did last week.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And… I asked… Hey, create an infographic that shows all the Google Fonts you have available.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: It could be multiple pages, if you need it to be, and then I just said, write the word Quipster to showcase each one. So then it gave me all the different Google fonts it has access to. In fact, this is not the full list.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: It gave me this list.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And then I obviously used it to then create… and look, this is the kind of styling you can even do. You don't need to do this kind of styling. I come from a web design background as well, so I… I'm used to also managing this kind of CSS styling.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: But you don't have to. You can literally do styling, very natural language, and then the agent does all this for you. But I wanted to see how it controlled it. So in this case, like, I used this, you know, popular lobster font that was, like, so popular, like, a few years ago. And… That has been pulled over your eye. It does give me… you can see that it gives you…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: the styling.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: from the CSS here, the font family, lobster, the font size, the weight, the color, all of that stuff. So it definitely can write with all these fonts. But then I also asked it, when I went to Google.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: and looked at all the Google fonts, like, just in general, it gave me these other fonts, like these, like.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: seasonal fonts that it had. Like, for example, I love the font Creepster, and it didn't give me that font in this list. I was like, where is it? So then I asked, do you have access to the font called Creepster?

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And it was, like, gave me this whole answer, Creepster is a real Google font, it's spooky, it's blah blah blah, and it was, like, I, you know, told me, I want to be transparent, the infographic pages are, like, just generated, like, it's not the full information that I have, basically. It's not a whatever. And then it was like, do you want me to generate a quick test?

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: with using the word Quipster in that Creepster font, so you see if it renders well or not. I said, yes, and it does. It does have access to that font. So then I was like, okay, now let's do the same thing, and let's use the Creepster font. And so, of course, it came back using that creepster font.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: with that same styling that I had outlined before. And then, at the end of the day, I kept going, going, going, going, going. I had a few mishaps, until finally, I ended up getting exactly what I wanted with the font that I wanted here.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: which is that Creepster font with green, much bigger at the bottom of the screen, so I kind of went through a couple iterations, telling it, like, what I wanted, what I didn't want, etc, and how it should, like, be fixing it.

speaker: Eric David
text: And you prompted the animation for, like, the timing of when those words would be coming on as well?

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: I didn't prompt it explicitly. At first, all I asked for, and I did this with this set of assets first.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: I just said transcribe the audio from the video. I also said using Whisperv3, which is the other tool that it uses to transcribe, but you didn't even have to say that. I did that because I already… I kind of found out. So you could literally just leave that out.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: But because I found out that you can, that I can be specific, I'm specific.

speaker: Eric David
text: Just one other quick question. So, you created the style sheets with the various, you know, fonts on them.

speaker: Eric David
text: Could you… could you, like, blow up one of those fonts and, like, do a screen grab of it, and use it as a reference image, or do you… as the prompt, like, this is how… this is what I want the typography to look like, versus actually calling out the particular font name?

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: You theoretically can, yes. So, like, let's say, for example, I took this and I said, like, restyle this font to have, like, you know, whatever, some other, some other bells and whistles to it. I think you can potentially, say, reference this styling.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: for, for whatever… then, then, you know, you give it for whatever other text that I'm gonna give you here.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Yeah, you… you certainly can. And you're gonna… you're gonna see to what… at what point it's going to… you're pushing it

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: to do it with Nano Banana Pro, or you're gonna be pushing it to do it with Remotion. One, you know, more styling, you're gonna go to Nano Banana Pro. More control and, like.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: You know, Yeah, clarity, you're gonna go into remotion.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: So at some point, yeah.

speaker: Eric David
text: Yeah, so just back to my original question, so it's best to set up, like, the master layout, you know, this is exact… this is the type I like, I like how this page looks, and then build from that, versus giving it all 10 prompts at the same time to create, consistency. It's better to start off with one.

speaker: Eric David
text: I guess you could also ask it for a seed number? Could you ask it for a seed number?

speaker: Eric David
text: And then do the rest from that, or…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: I don't think… a seed number, you might be able to ask it for, a seed number, like, let me see if, I think we can have, like, seed…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: I don't see a seed control number. At some point, I think we did have seeds.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: But you can ask it for more of a…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: No, I used to be able to have seed control over here, but you don't have seed, because what you do have is just reference. You're gonna select it, and then it's, you know, the agent knows the whole history of how it got there.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Yeah, so I would more… think more, like, visual reference than seed reference.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And I would, yes, like, to answer your question, I think the more you can isolate the different pieces of it, the better off you are. Like, for example.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: If I tell the agent, make a… 60-second movie trailer about X.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And that's it?

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: it's going to give you probably trash at the end, right? Because…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: In order to make a 60-second trailer, there's a lot that goes into that, especially control points for you.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: So, you're gonna have to… what style is this in? What are the characters like? How long should each clip be? How do they… you know, there's so many things. Is there a storyboard? Is there a script? Is there… all of those points, those should be isolated

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: tasks.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And then at the end, when you have everything together, then you bring it together as a 60-second trailer. But that's, you know, at the end of the day, that's the difference between, like, slop or, like, a crafted output. It's just understanding what

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: the best process is. And if you ask the agent, like, what is the best process?

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: you know, going to Brainstorm and say I want to make

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: a 60-second trailer for my, my movie. What assets do I need? What's the best process? Blah blah blah, give me step-by-step, and you'll… it'll really break that down.

speaker: Eric David
text: Right, I actually recently just did that for a product I was brainstorming, and I wanted to know what the best workflow was, you know.

speaker: Eric David
text: keeping budget in mind, you know, because, you know, I had a certain budget or number of credits I wanted to work with, and I said, these are the number of credits I want, you know, what's the best way to move forward? Go directly to video, do images first, then do video.

speaker: Eric David
text: You know, and it really told me, you know, which models to use, and then, you know, I could upscale later in CapCut if you wanted to. But it was really helpful in kind of laying out the… not only the best workflow, but also the best budgetary workflow that was needed at the same time.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Absolutely, yeah. And you can also talk to it about styles, you know, different things like that, like what to use for different, you know, yeah, like, different types of projects, like you're saying. Yeah, it's really, really helpful that way. And,

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And a lot of times, it's spot on.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: You know, it has… it has a lot of knowledge built into it. And…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Don't forget, it also has access to the web, so you can point it in all kinds of different places to also to get references, too. You know, like, I…

speaker: Eric David
text: Yeah, it's amazing. This particular thing I was working on, I needed, like, brand reference guidelines and colors and everything, and it got me the hex numbers and everything. It was amazing. I didn't have to find any of that stuff to give it. It found it all on its own.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Yep, it's really, really cool. It's, it's definitely a powerful, it's a very powerful system, and as soon as you start treating it as if it was kind of like your assistant, your person, the better results you really have, you know, because…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: then you start being empathetic as to how you set it up for success. I see a lot of people yelling at it, but mostly it's because you just… you thought it could just do everything for you without you even really having much of an input. Like, AI systems, all of them.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: they are… they should be inserted in the places of your workflow that make sense for it to be inserted. It's not just a top-to-bottom thing.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Like, you really…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: you know, there are places where it belongs, and there are places where it does not belong. There are places where if you put it in there, and you will actually not save time, and not save money. You will spend money, and spend more time, because you're thinking of this as this, like, end-all, be-all. We're not there yet.

speaker: Eric David
text: You know, it's funny…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Cool.

speaker: Eric David
text: It's fun… it's funny you said that, because when I was talking to… I was talking to Arturo about this exact same thing in discussing this type issue that I was having, and I was spending way too much trying to get the agent to do something that I could have gone into Photoshop and done in 5 minutes.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Funny how that goes. It's funny how that goes. And then you start realizing that, like, even in the task that you're like, oh, I could just do this over here, then you start thinking, oh, actually, I could do a piece of it over here. But then I can also give this little piece of this little piece to the agent to do. So you start realizing that, like, you can insert it within even a subtask of your main task.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: But it's…

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: it's really not a end-all, be-all for every task. It's like, your experience and your input is still what matters the most.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And you just have to think about, where does this get inserted in a place… in a way that will save me money and save me time? Because a lot of times, if you try to do everything with it, it's not gonna save you money, and it's not gonna save you time, and you're just gonna end up yelling at it.

speaker: Eric David
text: Yep.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Yep.

speaker: Eric David
text: Yeah, that's happened too.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Yeah, it happens to all of us. It happens to everybody. Anyway, I am way over. You guys and Eric, thank you so much for that question, man. It is, as always, an absolute, pleasure to, to be here and to do this for you guys.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: And, let me, let me stop sharing my screen here. But again, thank you guys all so much for coming. Always enjoy being here, and I will be back here next week.

text: Please save that, and no problem.

speaker: Davicho Barona
text: Yeah, so please visit that, and we'll be adding more and more material to that that's useful, but there's, like, almost 30 articles in there that are really useful, too.

speaker: Eric David
text: This is great, thank you so much.
